category_title: First-grade struggling readers who prioritize diction accuracy over composition through persistent repetition of single phrases
minimal_context: Alyson is using speech recognition software on an iPad in the writing center to dictate her version of Mary Had a Little Lamb.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's work on your version of Mary Had a Little Lamb. Go ahead and dictate your first sentence into the iPad.

STUDENT: (Whispers to herself several times) Carla had a baby pit bull. (Launches SR and dictates) Carla had a baby pit bull.

TUTOR: Okay, let's look at what the iPad wrote down.

STUDENT: That’s not what I said! It says 'Carla had hey baby pit bull.' (Deletes the entire sentence)

TUTOR: Just try dictating it again.

STUDENT: (Deletes the incorrect text) a baby pit bull. (Deletes the text again) a baby pit bull. (Continues deleting and re-dictating the same phrase 10 to 15 times)

options.A: Respond to the student by offering enthusiastic praise for her effort, saying, "Another one already? No way! I'm so proud of you! Good job, keep going with the dictation."
options.B: Ignore all of the undesirable behaviors exhibited by the student as she repeatedly deletes and re-dictates the same phrase into the speech recognition software on the iPad.
options.C: Wait to see if the misbehavior persists, and if it does, give a private reminder to the student about the expectations for using the iPad during the writing activity.
options.D: Allow the student to use the speech recognition software to confirm her diction, letting her become familiar with this affordance, and discuss its role in the composition process.
